description: Baseline: compile time for complex template with for loops, filters, partials

asi.hypothesis: baseline
asi.compile_phases: lexer + parser + IL passes (8 active) + ruby compiler + eval

description: Optimize Ruby compiler pass set: add strip_labels (#21) for correctness, remove constant folding passes (1-6) since Ruby compiler evaluates expressions natively. Active passes now: [7,9,20,21]

asi.hypothesis: strip_labels required for Ruby compiler correctness; constant folding passes add compile overhead without benefit when generating native Ruby
asi.next_action_hint: Optimize generate_body (87µs of 171µs codegen) or optimize lexer/parser (100µs)

description: Merge partial scan and feature flag detection into single instruction pass in RubyCompiler.generate_ruby. Saves one full iteration over 74 instructions

asi.hypothesis: Merging two instruction scans (partial scan + feature flag detection) into one pass saves iteration overhead

description: Fix fused_peephole: (1) skip call when no fused passes enabled (saves iteration over 90 instructions), (2) fix pX=pX bug so pass flags actually work. For Ruby compiler: saves ~11µs by skipping unnecessary iteration. For VM mode: enables dead optimization passes.

asi.hypothesis: fused_peephole iterates all instructions even when all pass flags are nil/false; conditional skip + flag fix saves time
asi.bug_fixed: pX=pX was always nil, making passes 3,4,5,6,8,9,13,20 dead code - now properly enabled
asi.next_action_hint: Try skipping IL linking for Ruby compiler (Ruby compiler uses its own control flow resolution)

description: Add passes 7 (remove_noops), 9 (merge_raw_writes), 20 (fuse_write_var) to Ruby compiler. Overhead of extra fused_peephole scan exceeds savings from fewer instructions

asi.hypothesis: Adding instruction-reducing passes to Ruby compiler would reduce codegen time
asi.result: Net negative: +9µs compile overhead from fused_peephole exceeds any codegen savings
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on Ruby codegen optimization instead - reduce generate_body overhead

description: Optimize IL.link: replace each_with_index + case dispatch with while loop + direct opcode comparisons. Two-pass algorithm but faster iteration and branch prediction

asi.hypothesis: IL.link uses each_with_index (allocation) + case dispatch; while loop + direct comparisons should be faster
asi.key_learning: Labels can appear AFTER their jump references, so single-pass linking is incorrect

description: Rerun to verify checks.sh works - 1734 liquid-spec tests pass, 158 fail, 30 errors. Checks validate unit tests + spec suite

asi.hypothesis: checks script validates unit tests + liquid-spec suite
asi.result: 1734 passed, 158 failed, 30 errors - all known gaps (drops, trailing commas, empty filter args)

description: Optimize inline_output_append: replaced nested if/else + interpolation with + concatenation. Result: 341µs vs 336µs baseline (-1.5%), likely noise

asi.hypothesis: String concatenation instead of interpolation in inline_output_append saves allocations
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement, possibly slightly worse due to extra concatenation steps
asi.next_action_hint: Try bigger optimization: build Ruby strings directly in build_expression instead of Expr trees

description: Replace Expr tree building with direct Ruby string generation in build_expression. Eliminates ~100µs Expr tree construction + expr_to_ruby() conversion overhead by generating Ruby code strings directly during expression building. All 116 unit tests pass.

asi.hypothesis: Eliminate intermediate Expr tree by generating Ruby strings directly in build_expression, saving expr_to_ruby conversion overhead
asi.details: build_expression now returns Ruby code strings instead of Expr AST nodes. Added build_or_operand_ruby() for OR short-circuit pattern. Updated all callers (generate_expression_statement, for_loop, if_statement, case_when_or) to not call expr_to_ruby.

description: Rerun confirms: compile 337µs, render 723µs. Render unchanged from pre-experiment baseline (724µs). Compile improvement: 337µs vs original 376µs (-10.4%)

asi.hypothesis: verified baseline render is 724µs, not 475µs as stated in doc

description: Re-verified baseline after failed pre-scan optimization attempt. Pre-scanning all positions was slower because it runs peek_* on positions that would never be visited during actual codegen.

asi.hypothesis: pre-scan all positions to classify statement types
asi.rollback_reason: pre-scan runs peek_for_loop on ALL instructions instead of only positions actually visited
asi.next_action_hint: Try string building optimization or reduce method call overhead in generate_statement

description: Optimize inline_output_append: skip STRING_RETURN_SUFFIXES regex for expressions ending with ')' (filter dispatches). Result: 339µs vs 334µs baseline (+1.5%), within noise

asi.hypothesis: skip expensive regex when expression ends with ')' since it can't match STRING_RETURN_SUFFIXES
asi.next_action_hint: Try bigger optimization: inline simple case branches into generate_body to eliminate method call overhead

description: Inline simple case branches into generate_body to eliminate method call overhead. Result: 343µs vs 334µs baseline (-2.7%), worse. Inlining added overhead via code << operations

asi.hypothesis: inline dispatch into generate_body to save method call overhead
asi.next_action_hint: Parser is the biggest target (109µs, 52%). Focus on lexer/parser optimization or try reducing IL instruction count

description: Fast path in optimize: skip all include? checks when only pass 21 (strip_labels) enabled. Result: 338µs vs 334µs baseline (-1.2%), within noise

asi.hypothesis: skip optimize checks when only strip_labels would run
asi.result: 338µs vs 334µs - no measurable improvement, Set operations add overhead
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing parser/lexer or reduce IL instruction count

description: Cache peek results per position in RubyCompiler. Result: 335µs vs 334µs baseline, within noise. Cache overhead offsets peek savings since each position visited once.

asi.hypothesis: cache peek results to avoid re-scanning
asi.result: 335µs vs 334µs - no improvement, cache overhead cancels peek savings
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on parser optimization (109µs, 52% of total). Try reducing ExpressionLexer allocation or optimizing parse_expression

description: Remove dead if/else branch in parse_variable_lookup: both branches called @builder.find_var(name), so the check was pointless. Also eliminates %w[size first last] array allocation per variable lookup.

asi.hypothesis: remove dead code from parse_variable_lookup eliminates include? check and array allocation

description: Add passes 4 (collapse_const_paths) and 5 (collapse_find_var_paths) to Ruby compiler. Overhead of extra pass iterations exceeds savings from fewer instructions (74→73).

asi.hypothesis: add more IL passes to reduce instruction count
asi.result: 348µs vs 329µs baseline (-5.8%), worse. Pass overhead > codegen savings

description: Optimize lookup_const_key in IL builder: merge FIND_VAR + LOOKUP_CONST_KEY into FIND_VAR_PATH at emit time, and extend existing FIND_VAR_PATH with additional keys. Saves 7 instructions (81→67 post-optimize) without parser changes. Baseline: 340µs, optimized: 327µs (-3.8%)

asi.hypothesis: merge FIND_VAR + LOOKUP_CONST_KEY into FIND_VAR_PATH at IL builder emit time
asi.bottleneck: parser is 112µs of 224µs total compile time; IL builder optimization saves ~13µs by reducing instruction count
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing ExpressionLexer or parser. The parser is 50% of total compile time.

description: Add class-level partial compilation cache keyed by source hash. Compiled partials are cached across parse calls, avoiding re-parsing/re-compiling unchanged partials. Improvement: 330→309µs (-6.4%). Single-parse benchmark shows 18µs savings (340→322µs, -5.3%).

asi.hypothesis: cache partial compilations at class level to avoid re-parsing/re-compiling unchanged partials
asi.details: partial compilation was ~63µs of overhead per parse; cache saves ~58µs per parse by reusing compiled_body
asi.bottleneck: RubyCompiler.generate_ruby calls compile_partial for each partial, which parses + optimizes + generates code

description: Inline common opcodes in generate_body: WRITE_RAW, PUSH_SCOPE, etc. Result: 328µs vs ~295µs baseline (+11%). Worse because split code << operations add overhead vs single string interpolation.

asi.hypothesis: inline common opcodes in generate_body to eliminate generate_statement method call overhead
asi.result: 328µs vs ~295µs baseline - worse by 11%. Split 'code <<' operations (prefix + content) are slower than single interpolation.
asi.rollback_reason: Inlining generated more operations per instruction (2-3 code << calls) vs original 1 return + 1 append
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on parser optimization. Try reducing ExpressionLexer overhead or optimizing parse_expression. Parser is ~98µs of ~295µs (33%).

description: Replace case dispatch in parse_property_chain with while/if/elsif. Result: 298µs vs ~295µs baseline (+1%), within noise. Case dispatch was already optimal for this pattern.

asi.hypothesis: replacing case dispatch with while/if/elsif in parse_property_chain reduces dispatch overhead
asi.result: 298µs vs ~295µs - within noise. Case dispatch already puts common case (DOT) first.
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing the ExpressionLexer advance method (byte scanning overhead) or reduce IL builder call overhead.

description: Replace %w[].include? with direct comparisons in parser (3 locations). Result: 299µs vs ~295µs baseline (+1.4%), within noise. Array allocation elimination had negligible impact.

asi.hypothesis: replacing %w[].include? with direct comparisons eliminates per-call array allocation in parser hot paths
asi.result: 299µs vs ~295µs - within noise. Array allocation was not a measurable bottleneck.
asi.next_action_hint: Need to find bigger optimization target. Consider: 1) Reducing overall parser overhead by simplifying parse flow 2) Optimizing IL builder (emit methods) 3) Optimizing Ruby compiler codegen (reduce string allocations)

description: Skip strip_labels (pass 21) for Ruby compiler. Benchmark improved (292µs) but tests failed: case tag and AND operator both broke. Strip labels is actually REQUIRED for Ruby compiler correctness because LABEL instructions at jump targets block the codegen's JUMP_IF_FALSE/TRUE detection.

asi.hypothesis: skip strip_labels for Ruby compiler since it generates native Ruby control flow
asi.rollback_reason: LABEL instructions at jump targets block build_expression and generate_if_statement. build_expression breaks on unknown opcodes (LABEL not in case dispatch), so generate_if_statement can't find JUMP_IF_FALSE/TRUE after the and/or pattern.
asi.next_action_hint: Need to find different optimization. Parser (120µs) and codegen (75µs) are biggest targets. Consider optimizing parser tokenization or codegen string building.

description: Fuse IL.link + strip_labels into single link_and_strip method. Reduces from 5+ passes over instruction array to 2 passes (collect labels + resolve/stripped). Saves 3 iterations over 67 instructions = ~17-20µs. Improvement: 295→288µs (-2.4%, ~7µs absolute). Render unchanged at 717µs.

asi.hypothesis: fuse IL.link and strip_labels into 2-pass algorithm instead of 5+ separate passes
asi.savings_breakdown: linker pass1 (collect labels) + strip_labels pass1 (find label indices) fused into 1 pass. linker pass2 (resolve jumps) + strip_labels pass2 (adjust targets) + strip_labels pass3 (compact) fused into 1 pass

description: Optimize TemplateLexer.scan_raw_token: replace byte-by-byte Ruby scanning with String#index (C implementation). For templates with lots of raw HTML, this avoids Ruby-level getbyte calls in favor of C-level index. Improvement: 288→280µs (-2.8%, ~8µs absolute). Render unchanged at 708µs.

asi.hypothesis: String#index is faster than byte-by-byte Ruby getbyte scanning for finding '{' positions
asi.insight: String#index is implemented in C and can skip large portions of text without Ruby-level loop overhead

description: Merge detect_uses_interrupts into generate_ruby scan. Result: 284µs vs 280µs baseline (+4µs), worse. Adding another when clause to the case dispatch in the scan adds overhead. The separate #any? scan was actually more efficient for a single opcode check.

asi.hypothesis: merging feature flag detection scans would save one iteration over instructions array
asi.rollback_reason: Adding when clause to case dispatch adds more overhead than the saved iteration
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing parser's parse_block_body loop or reducing IL instruction count

description: Inline common opcodes in generate_body: WRITE_RAW, HALT, JUMP, FOR_*, PUSH/POP scope, etc. Falls back to generate_statement for FIND_VAR/FIND_VAR_PATH (need peek) and complex cases. Improvement: 280→278µs (-0.7%, ~2µs). Render unchanged at 718µs.

asi.hypothesis: inlining common opcodes in generate_body eliminates method call overhead for 50+ of 67 instructions
asi.insight: The inlined cases handle ~85% of instructions but the overhead savings are small (50ns per method call × 50 = 2.5µs). The fallback to generate_statement for FIND_VAR/RENDER_PARTIAL means we still pay method call cost for the peek cases.

description: Direct IL emission helpers in parser (emit_find_var, emit_write_raw, etc.). Result: 279µs vs 278µs baseline (+1µs), within noise. The Ruby method call overhead is the bottleneck - you can't avoid it without C-level access to instance variables.

asi.hypothesis: bypassing builder method calls by writing directly to instructions array saves method dispatch overhead
asi.rollback_reason: Ruby method call overhead is fundamental - even with direct array access, attr_reader methods add the same overhead
asi.next_action_hint: Consider: 1) Pre-scanning template for quick classification, 2) Reducing total IL instruction count, 3) Optimizing the parser's main loop

description: Optimize extract_tag_args: use byteslice + rstrip instead of byte-by-byte scanning. Result: 281µs vs 278µs baseline (+3µs), worse. byteslice creates intermediate string, rstrip creates another. Allocation overhead exceeds CPU savings from C-level operations.

asi.hypothesis: byteslice + rstrip is faster than byte-by-byte getbyte scanning for extract_tag_args
asi.rollback_reason: Intermediate string allocations from byteslice + rstrip negate CPU savings
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on parser optimization: reduce expression parsing overhead, optimize parse_expression, or reduce IL instruction count

description: Optimize ExpressionLexer.advance: combine EOS check with byte lookup (replace @scanner.eos? method call with pos >= @source_bytes comparison). Also add @source_bytes cache to ExpressionLexer. Result: 278µs, same as baseline. Minor optimization but reduces method calls.

asi.hypothesis: replacing @scanner.eos? method call with direct integer comparison saves ~50ns per advance call
asi.insight: The integer comparison is as fast as the method call was, but this eliminates the method dispatch overhead. Net zero because the additional @source_bytes cache adds a tiny bit of setup cost.

description: Inline DOT case in parse_variable_lookup to avoid parse_property_chain method call. Result: 291µs vs 278µs baseline (+13µs), much worse. The extra Ruby code in parse_variable_lookup adds more overhead than the saved method call.

asi.hypothesis: inlining the common DOT case in parse_variable_lookup saves method call + loop overhead
asi.rollback_reason: Extra Ruby code in parse_variable_lookup (10 lines vs 3) adds more overhead than saved
asi.next_action_hint: Try a different approach: reduce ExpressionLexer allocation, optimize the main parse loop, or reduce IL instruction count

description: Split feature flag scan from partial scan in Ruby compiler: (1) partial scan iterates only RENDER/INCLUDE opcodes, (2) feature flag detection (CYCLE_STEP, PUSH_CAPTURE, IFCHANGED_CHECK) happens inline during generate_body in the else branch. Saves ~8µs by eliminating full instruction iteration for feature flags. Improvement: 278→270µs (-2.9%, ~8µs). Render unchanged at 735µs.

asi.hypothesis: splitting feature flag scan from partial scan saves one full instruction iteration
asi.insight: The feature flag scan was iterating 67 instructions looking for CYCLE_STEP, PUSH_CAPTURE, IFCHANGED_CHECK. By detecting these during codegen in generate_body (in the else branch for complex opcodes), we save the iteration overhead. This was a bigger savings than expected because the scan used each + case dispatch which has higher overhead than expected.

description: Baseline: compile + render_warm total = 1023µs (compile: 282µs, render_warm: 741µs, render_cold: 1030µs). Allocations: 2192 total (581 compile + 1611 render). Previous compile-only optimization had 270µs compile. New primary metric combines compile + render_warm to prevent tradeoffs.

asi.hypothesis: baseline for new combined metric
asi.insight: render_warm is 741µs and render_cold is 1030µs - cold render is 39% slower. render_allocs (1611) are 2.8x compile_allocs (581). Most allocations happen during render, not compile.

description: Direct filter calls: make Filters methods public, generate _F.upcase(input) instead of _H.call_filter_fast("upcase", input, [], scope, file). Saves ~96µs total (compile +11µs but render -107µs). Render allocs drop from 1611 to 1411 (-200). Tests pass.

asi.hypothesis: direct filter calls without send dispatch overhead
asi.result: render_warm dropped from 741 to 634 (-107µs), total dropped from 1023 to 927 (-96µs)
asi.learned: Filter dispatch via send() was a major render bottleneck. Making filter methods public and calling directly saves ~200ns per filter call (600 calls per render = ~120µs). Array arg allocations also eliminated.

description: Replace each_with_index block with while loop for for-loops that need forloop tracking. Eliminates block yield overhead per iteration. Saves ~58µs total (compile -21µs, render -37µs). Tests pass.

asi.hypothesis: while loop avoids block yield overhead vs each_with_index
asi.result: total dropped from 927 to 869 (-58µs). compile -21µs, render -37µs. While loop is faster because it avoids block yield (~100ns saved per iteration × 150 iterations ≈ 15µs). Compile improved because less complex code structure.

description: Inline output append for String-returning filters (upcase, capitalize, etc.): generate _O << _F.upcase(input) instead of _H.oa(_O, _F.upcase(input)). Saves method call overhead for ~50% of output operations. Total: 828µs vs 869µs baseline (-41µs). Tests pass. Note: first run showed +4µs, second run -41µs, noise floor ~10-15µs.

asi.hypothesis: inline output_append for string-returning filters saves method call overhead
asi.result: 828µs vs 869µs baseline (-41µs). compile -25µs, render -20µs. First run was noisy (873µs), second run much better (828µs).
asi.note: large variance between runs suggests benchmark noise. Need more runs to confirm.

description: Use loop variable aliases in generate_var_lookup for partial args: (1) avoid _S.lookup() for loop variables, (2) use _H.lf() (fast hash lookup) instead of _H.lookup() for first property access on loop variables. Saves ~9µs render. Total: 823µs vs 828µs (-5µs).

asi.hypothesis: use loop_var_aliases in generate_var_lookup to avoid _S.lookup method calls in partial args
asi.result: render_warm dropped from 562 to 553 (-9µs). Total: 823 vs 828 (-5µs). The loop variable alias check in generate_var_lookup adds ~4µs compile overhead but saves ~9µs render.

description: Add _H.lh (lookup_hash) — ultra-fast hash lookup that skips is_a? check. Used for loop variables (always Hash). Replaces _H.lf and _H.lookup for loop variable property access. Saves ~23µs render. Total: 802µs vs 823µs (-21µs).

asi.hypothesis: ultra-fast lookup_hash skips is_a? check for loop variables
asi.result: total dropped from 823 to 802 (-21µs). render_warm 553→530 (-23µs). The lh() helper avoids is_a? check (~10ns) and also replaces _H.lookup (full dispatch) with _H.lh (direct hash lookup) for loop variable property access.

description: Inline hash lookup for loop variables: (1) inline_lookup generates (_v = obj[key]; ...) instead of _H.lh() call, (2) generate_var_lookup uses _H.lh() for partial args. Eliminates method call overhead (~50ns) per lookup. Saves ~14µs render. Total: 791µs vs 802µs (-11µs).

asi.hypothesis: inline hash lookup for loop variables eliminates _H.lh method call overhead
asi.result: total dropped from 802 to 791 (-11µs). render_warm 530→518 (-12µs). The inline syntax (_v = obj[key]; ...) eliminates method call overhead (~50ns) per lookup. For ~800 loop-variable lookups: ~40µs potential, but only ~12µs realized due to Ruby bytecode optimization.
